overall_summary: EPSA, representing competitive power suppliers and independent power producers, broadly supports the DOE ANOPR and urges FERC to act quickly on both the rulemaking and pending proceedings like the PJM Show Cause. The filing endorses FERC's jurisdictional authority over large load interconnections to the interstate transmission system and calls for a transparent, standardized, and expedited process that protects competitive market principles and avoids discriminating against new loads or existing generators.

quotes.text: rules of the road are necessary to provide sufficient market certainty and the transparency needed to enable large loads to interconnect to the transmission system efficiently and fairly, and to be able to do so quickly.
quotes.concern: standardized rules needed for market certainty and transparency in large load interconnection

quotes.text: It would be entirely inconsistent with the intent of the ANOPR to further delay resolution of the Show Cause proceeding to clarify the rules applicable to large load interconnections in PJM, which has been awaiting Commission action based on an extensive record for over six months.
quotes.concern: further delay of the PJM Show Cause proceeding would contradict the ANOPR's goals

quotes.text: existing projects that have reached advanced interconnection milestones should be afforded legacy-based treatment in order to rely on the framework under which their commercial arrangement has made substantial progress.
quotes.concern: projects with advanced milestones need legacy treatment during transition

quotes.text: interconnection procedures and agreements for large loads require a level of standardization to establish certainty and transparency – both of which are sorely lacking today in the opaque and unfiled individual Transmission Owner processes that exacerbate confusion and delay regarding new load connections while furthering uncertainty by establishing load forecasts inflated by speculative projects.
quotes.concern: current Transmission Owner processes are opaque and create inflated load forecasts

quotes.text: establishing a clear, expedited glide path for large load customer interconnections to the transmission system should bring visibility to committed new demand, shining a light on how much of forecasted demand growth is ready and able to join the system.
quotes.concern: a clear interconnection path would improve visibility of real committed demand versus speculative projections

quotes.text: The Secretary correctly recognizes the Commission's jurisdiction to regulate the interconnection of large loads to the interstate transmission grid.
quotes.concern: EPSA affirms FERC's jurisdictional authority over large load interconnections

quotes.text: the issue is whether the interconnection service is in interstate commerce, not whether that commerce involves a wholesale sale by an interconnecting generator or a retail purchase by an interconnecting large load.
quotes.concern: jurisdiction turns on whether interconnection service is in interstate commerce, not on the nature of the sale

quotes.text: This would result in substantial uncertainty for loads, which would have to wait for classification decisions made by self-interested utilities to determine whether they fall into the State "local distribution" bucket or the Federal "transmission" bucket.
quotes.concern: fact-based seven-factor test for facility classification creates uncertainty and delay

quotes.text: it is important that the Commission clarify that the established threshold is based on the full expected load, even if a large load customer ramps up to that amount in phases.
quotes.concern: threshold should apply to full projected load, not initial phase capacity

quotes.text: we need a transparent, workable process that resolves the problems of the existing transmission owner processes which are not subject to open access principles.
quotes.concern: queue process must be transparent and subject to open access, not controlled by transmission owners

quotes.text: EPSA firmly agrees that, when a large load customer agrees to be curtailable and dispatchable in order to assist in system reliability and flexibility in defined circumstances, that load should be subject to an expedited interconnection study process.
quotes.concern: curtailable and dispatchable loads should receive expedited study treatment

quotes.text: the Commission should direct ISOs/RTOs to complete studies within a specific time – bundled on a concurrent timeline with any transmission owner studies to the extent possible.
quotes.concern: study timelines should be concurrent and time-bound, not serial

quotes.text: EPSA rejects the underlying premise that existing generation that is moved behind the meter to serve data center load is akin to a partial suspension or otherwise warrants RMR/SSR type analysis for deactivating resources.
quotes.concern: behind-the-meter generation for data centers should not trigger RMR or SSR deactivation analysis

quotes.text: Any reliability assessment for co-located or hybrid arrangements must be limited to whether and how the proposed new load can be reliably and safely interconnected to the grid.
quotes.concern: reliability review for co-located loads must be scoped to interconnection safety, not resource adequacy

quotes.text: To introduce resource adequacy as a consideration in determining whether and at what cost to interconnect new large load would necessarily discriminate against that new load and existing generators.
quotes.concern: using resource adequacy as an interconnection cost criterion would be discriminatory

bins.name: FERC Jurisdiction Affirmed
bins.description: EPSA supports FERC's authority to regulate large load interconnections to the interstate transmission system. The filing argues jurisdiction turns on whether the interconnection service is in interstate commerce, and criticizes the seven-factor test for facility classification as creating delay and self-interested utility decision-making.

bins.name: Threshold Based on Full Load
bins.description: EPSA asks the Commission to clarify that the 20 MW threshold applies to the full expected load, not just the initial phase, so that phased ramp-up projects are captured from the start.

bins.name: Expedited Study for Flexible Loads
bins.description: EPSA strongly supports expedited interconnection study processes for curtailable and dispatchable loads. It calls for concurrent, time-bound study timelines across ISOs, RTOs, and transmission owners rather than serial processes that have historically caused delay.

bins.name: Hybrid and Co-located Scope Limits
bins.description: EPSA supports interconnection rights for hybrid and co-located facilities but opposes extending reliability reviews beyond safe interconnection to include resource adequacy, which it views as discriminatory against new loads and existing generators.

bins.name: Standardized Transparent Study Process
bins.description: EPSA supports a standardized, open-access interconnection study process to replace opaque, unfiled Transmission Owner procedures. It urges the Commission to complete the PJM Show Cause proceeding and to protect advanced in-flight projects through legacy treatment during any transition.

bins.name: Flexibility Commitment Earns Expedited Access
bins.description: EPSA agrees that load customers willing to commit to curtailability and dispatchability should receive expedited interconnection study treatment, consistent with the ANOPR's principle on flexible loads.

bins.name: Co-location Review Scope Limited
bins.description: EPSA rejects applying RMR or SSR analysis to generation moved behind the meter to serve data center load. It argues that reliability assessments for co-located or hybrid arrangements must be limited to safe interconnection, and that introducing resource adequacy criteria would discriminate against new loads and existing generators.

bins.name: Resource Adequacy Must Not Drive Cost
bins.description: EPSA opposes using resource adequacy as a basis for determining interconnection cost, arguing it would be discriminatory under open access principles.

bins.name: Resolve PJM Show Cause Now
bins.description: EPSA repeatedly urges FERC to act on the pending PJM Show Cause proceeding, arguing that further delay is inconsistent with the ANOPR's intent to ensure timely and orderly large load interconnection.

bins.name: Market Certainty and Transparency
bins.description: EPSA emphasizes that the primary gap is the absence of clear rules giving market participants sufficient certainty to plan and invest. A standardized process would provide the transparency that both developers and system operators need.

bins.name: Pending Proceedings Must Advance
bins.description: EPSA argues the ANOPR should compel FERC to complete pending proceedings with mature records, such as the PJM Show Cause, rather than let the rulemaking timeline become a reason for additional delay.

bins.name: Legacy Treatment for Advanced Projects
bins.description: EPSA asks that projects already far along in existing interconnection processes receive legacy-based treatment so new rules do not disrupt commercial arrangements nearing commercialization.

bins.name: Opacity of Transmission Owner Processes
bins.description: EPSA characterizes existing load interconnection queues as opaque, unfiled, and entirely within Transmission Owner control, and uses this to justify the need for FERC-mandated open access standards.

bins.name: Visibility of Real Committed Demand
bins.description: EPSA argues that a clear interconnection process would separate real, committed load from speculative forecasts, improving planning accuracy for generators and system operators.

bins.name: Bright-line Facility Classification
bins.description: EPSA recommends the Commission adopt bright-line voltage-based facility classification rather than the seven-factor test, which it says will generate litigation and delay from self-interested utility decisions.

bins.name: Open Access Queue Required
bins.description: EPSA calls for interconnection queues subject to open access principles, replacing the current Transmission Owner-controlled processes that lack transparency and non-discriminatory standards.

bins.name: RMR and SSR Analysis Inappropriate
bins.description: EPSA rejects applying deactivation-style RMR or SSR analysis to generation that moves behind the meter to serve data centers, arguing such resources may still serve the grid and the analogy is inapt.
